# Quickwit 0.5: Distributed tracing with Open Telemetry and Jaeger, VRL, Pulsar support, and more...

*   [![Paul Masurel](https://github.com/fulmicoton.png)Paul Masurel](https://github.com/fulmicoton)

March 17, 2023

![Quickwit 0.5 release - Open telemetry, Jaeger, VRL, Pulsar support, and more.](/img/blog/quickwit-0.5-release.webp)

Today, we are thrilled to announce the release of Quickwit 0.5.0! This new version marks a turning point in the history of the project. In addition to the usual "horizontal" new features and improvements, this release accommodates a developer-facing use case from start to finish: Quickwit 0.5 is waiting to back your distributed tracing data!

Let's unpack this!

# Distributed tracing with Jaeger and OpenTelemetry

Probably the freshest face of three pillars of observability, distributed tracing is already helping a lot of developers to understand and debug their distributed systems. The most popular solution is called [Jaeger](https://www.jaegertracing.io/). Its iconic UI displays all the RPC calls involved in a given request on a single screen.

![](/img/blog/2023-03-17-jaeger.png)

Unfortunately, while Jaeger can work standalone, it does not offer any persistence. To deal with a large amount of data or if you want your requests to stick around, you will likely need to plug Jaeger to Elasticsearch or Cassandra. These two solutions are solid and great, but they are also notoriously hard to deploy and manage.

Starting in Quickwit 0.5, you will also have the choice to use Quickwit as your Jaeger backend. You can configure Quickwit to store its data and its metadata on your object storage (for instance Amazon S3). Now that replicaton is off the table, you can comfortably spin a single quickwit node, to index and search all of your traces.

Object storage services are incredibly affordable, with price varying at around $25 per TB / month. With such a low cost, you can afford to be generous when setting the retention period for your traces. At some point in the future, you may find yourself revisiting an issue that was left hanging in your bug tracker for a long time. You will thank yourself for the longer retention!

![](/img/blog/2023-03-17-distributed-tracing-overview.png)

The easiest way to ship your traces to Quickwit is via OpenTelemetry. Quickwit 0.5 indeed comes with an OpenTelemetry GRPC endpoint. You can either plug the OpenTelemetry Collector to Quickwit, or you can configure your application to ship the logs directly to Quickwit.

Once this is done, all that is left is to start jaeger with the grpc plugin storage pointing to quickwit.

A tutorial is available [here](/docs/distributed-tracing/plug-quickwit-to-jaeger).

# C

content.text:
Got it — Quickwit is confirmed. Two important corrections to what you described earlier though:

**Quickwit does NOT use ClickHouse.** It uses **object storage (S3/MinIO/GCS)** directly — same fundamental approach as OpenObserve, just a different search engine underneath (Apache Tantivy). It's architecturally closer to OpenObserve than to SigNoz.

What Quickwit 0.5 actually introduced was: Quickwit as a **Jaeger storage backend**. Instead of plugging Jaeger into Elasticsearch or Cassandra (which are heavy), you point Jaeger at Quickwit, and Quickwit stores the trace data cheaply on object storage. The OTel collector sends data to Quickwit, Jaeger reads it back.

```
Your services (OTel SDK)
    → OTel Collector → Quickwit (stores traces on S3)
                            ↑
                       Jaeger UI reads via gRPC storage plugin
```

So it's not "Jaeger on ClickHouse" — it's "Jaeger UI on top of Quickwit on top of S3."

---

## Revised Observability Comparison

| | Quickwit | OpenObserve | SigNoz |
|---|---|---|---|
| Storage | Object storage (S3) | Parquet on S3/local disk | ClickHouse |
| Jaeger UI | Yes (native backend) | No (Jaeger protocol only, no Jaeger UI plugin) | No (own UI) |
| All 3 signals | Logs + Traces (metrics beta) | Yes | Yes |
| Written in | Rust | Rust | Go |
| Memory footprint | Low | Very low | High (ClickHouse) |
| **Status** | **Acquired by Datadog** | Active, independent | Active, independent |

The Datadog acquisition is the dealbreaker for Quickwit in Raven's context. Datadog acquired them to absorb the technology — the project's independent roadmap is effectively dead. Building a self-hosted enterprise product on top of an acquired open-source project is a known risk: features get paywalled, community support evaporates, or the repo gets archived.

**The observability recommendation stays unchanged:**
- Edge/self-hosted → **OpenObserve** (single binary, S3-compatible, same object storage approach as Quickwit but healthier project)
- Cloud/enterprise → **SigNoz** (ClickHouse, full signals, active independent project)

Quickwit's only unique angle was the native Jaeger UI integration, but OpenObserve + SigNoz both handle distributed tracing natively via OTel — you don't need a separate Jaeger UI if you have their own trace explorer UIs.

---

Now — should I update the research doc to capture all of this (Quickwit clarification, QBit re-evaluation, ClickHouse for vectors revised verdict) and then we go into full brainstorming mode for the **enterprise connectors** system? That's a meaty feature that deserves a proper spec.

content.text:
**On ClickHouse + QBit for vectors — yes, it makes sense for Raven's trajectory.** Here's why I've revised my position:

1. **SigNoz already brings ClickHouse into the stack** — so ClickHouse isn't an additional operational burden, it's already there
2. **QBit eliminates the RAM ceiling** — no HNSW index to hold in memory, reads directly from columnar storage
3. **Your queries are always scoped** — every RAG query hits `WHERE org_id = X AND kb_id = Y`. With ClickHouse partitioning on those keys, QBit only scans that partition, not the whole table. A 500K chunk knowledge base scans in under 300ms.
4. **Unified analytics + vectors** — "which chunks get retrieved most?" is a single JOIN, not a cross-system query

The honest ceiling: if a single knowledge base exceeds ~5–10M chunks (an entire university library), QBit's O(n) starts to hurt. But that's a Phase 4+ problem, and by then you can add Qdrant as a hot layer for those specific mega-KBs while keeping ClickHouse as the default.

**Decision captured:** pgvector for MVP/Phase 1 → ClickHouse + QBit for Phase 2+ enterprise scale → Qdrant only if a single KB exceeds 10M chunks.

---

Now, brainstorming the **Enterprise Connector System**. First clarifying question:

When you say "take it to your own premise" — are you envisioning this as:

**(A)** The Raven platform deployed inside the client's infrastructure, connecting to their internal data sources (databases, file stores) that never leave their network — like a Palantir/Foundry model

**(B)** A hybrid where Raven runs in your cloud but reaches into the client's systems via secure tunnels/agents (like Fivetran's agent model)

**(C)** Both — enterprise gets the on-prem option, mid-market gets the cloud-with-agent option

This shapes the entire security model and connector architecture.

message.content: Actually, I would like to go with option C.

content.text:
Good call — that covers both ends of the market.

Next question: For the connector system, are you thinking of building a **generic connector framework** where you write connector adapters yourself (PostgreSQL connector, S3 connector, WordPress connector, etc.), or are you open to **embedding Airbyte as the data movement engine** and focusing Raven's effort on the post-ingestion layer (chunking, embedding, classification, knowledge graph routing)?

The tradeoff:

**(A)** Build your own connector framework — full control, lighter weight, but you're maintaining 20+ connectors long-term (schema changes, API versioning, auth flows per source)

**(B)** Embed Airbyte (open-source, MIT core) — 700+ connectors on day one, community maintains them, but adds a dependency and Airbyte's own resource footprint

**(C)** Hybrid — use Airbyte for the long tail of SaaS/cloud sources (Salesforce, HubSpot, Google Drive, etc.) but build native connectors for the high-value database/file sources (PostgreSQL, MySQL, S3, SharePoint) where you want tighter control and lower overhead

On this page

## Goals[​](#goals "Direct link to Goals")

The Airbyte Protocol describes a series of standard components and all the interactions between them in order to declare an ELT pipeline. All message passing across components is done via serialized JSON messages for inter-process communication.

This document describes the protocol as it exists in its CURRENT form. Stay tuned for an RFC on how the protocol will evolve.

This document is intended to contain ALL the rules of the Airbyte Protocol in one place. Anything not contained in this document is NOT part of the Protocol. At the time of writing, there is one known exception, which is the [Supported Data Types](/platform/understanding-airbyte/supported-data-types), which contains rules on data types that are part of the Protocol. That said, there are additional articles, e.g. [A Beginner's Guide to the Airbyte Catalog](/platform/understanding-airbyte/beginners-guide-to-catalog) that repackage the information in this document for different audiences.

## Key Concepts[​](#key-concepts "Direct link to Key Concepts")

There are 2 major components in the Airbyte Protocol: Source and Destination. These components are referred to as Actors. A source is an application that is described by a series of standard interfaces. This application extracts data from an underlying data store. A data store in this context refers to the tool where the data is actually stored. A data store includes: databases, APIs, anything that produces data, etc. For example, the Postgres Source is a Source that pulls from Postgres (which is a data store). A Destination is an application that is described by a series of standard interfaces that loads data into a data store.

The key primitives that the Protocol uses to describe data are Catalog, Configured Catalog, Stream, Configured Stream, and Field:

*   Stream - A Stream describes the schema of a resource and various metadata about how a user can interact with that resource. A resource in this context might refer to a database table, a resource in a REST API, or a data stream.
*   Field - A Field refers to a "column" in a Stream. In a database this would be a column; in a JSON object it is a field.
*   Catalog - A Catalog is a list of Streams that describes the data in the data store that a Source represents.

An Actor can advertise information about itself with an [Actor Specification](#actor-specification). One of the main pieces of information the specification shares is what information is needed to configure an Actor.

Each of these concepts is described in greater depth in their respective section.

content.text:
## Airbyte source connector PostgreSQL CDC incremental sync

--- [Airbyte protocol docs] ---
### Actor Specification[​](#actor-specification "Direct link to Actor Specification")

…on[​](#actor-specification "Direct link to Actor Specification")

The specification allows the Actor to share information about itself.

The `connectionSpecification` is [JSONSchema](https://json-schema.org) that describes what information needs to the actor for it operate. e.g. If using a Postgres Source, the `ConnectorSpecification` would specify that a `hostname`, `port`, and `password` are required in order for the connector to function. This JSONSchema can be used to validate that the provided inputs are valid. e.g. If `port` is one of the fields and the JsonSchema in the `connectionSpecification` specifies that this field should be a number, if a user inputs "airbyte", they will receive an error. For connection specification, Airbyte adheres to JsonSchema validation rules. The Airbyte implementation of the Protocol is able to render this JSONSchema to produce a form for users to fill in the information for an Actor.

The specification also contains information about what features the Actor supports.

*   `protocol_version` describes which version of the protocol the Connector supports. The default value is "0.2.0".
*   `supported_destination_sync_modes` - describes which sync modes a destination is able to support. See [Sync Modes](#source-sync-modes).

`documentationUrl` and `changelogUrl` are optional fields that link to additional information about the connector.

content.text:
Concrete tradeoffs for the Postgres example:

**Airbyte Postgres connector:**
- Handles CDC (Change Data Capture) via WAL — detects inserts/updates/deletes automatically
- Handles incremental sync (only new/changed rows since last sync)
- Handles schema discovery (reads table structures, column types)
- Handles 50+ edge cases: SSL modes, SSH tunnels, logical replication slots, custom schemas
- **But:** each connector runs as a separate Docker container. Airbyte's platform itself needs ~2–4 GB RAM (scheduler, API server, temporal workflow engine, database). A sync job spins up source + destination containers temporarily.

**Native Raven Postgres connector:**
- You write a Go service: `sql.Open("postgres", dsn)` → query → chunk → embed. Maybe 200 lines of code.
- Lightweight — no extra containers, no Airbyte platform overhead
- **But:** you now own CDC, incremental sync, schema drift, connection pooling, SSH tunneling, SSL cert management. That's where the 200 lines becomes 2,000+ and keeps growing.

**The real question is: how many connectors will enterprise clients need?**

If it's 3–5 (Postgres, MySQL, S3, SharePoint), building native is cleaner and lighter. If it's 15+ (add Snowflake, BigQuery, Salesforce, HubSpot, Confluence, Google Drive, Notion, Jira, MongoDB, Oracle, MSSQL...), maintaining that is a full engineering team's job.

My recommendation is **(C) Hybrid** — but weighted differently than you might expect:

- **Build native connectors for databases** (Postgres, MySQL, ClickHouse) — these are simple `SELECT` queries over a Go SQL driver, your ingestion pipeline already speaks Go, and you avoid the Airbyte platform overhead for on-prem clients where resources matter
- **Use Airbyte for SaaS/cloud sources** (Salesforce, Google Drive, Confluence, SharePoint, HubSpot) — these APIs are auth nightmares (OAuth2 flows, refresh tokens, pagination, rate limits, API version deprecation) that you absolutely do not want to maintain

Does that split make sense for what you're envisioning? Or do you see the database connectors also needing CDC-level complexity (real-time sync as client data changes)?

message.content: Let's not go with native connectors to begin with. Let's just rely on Airbyte. I understand that the CDC will be handled by the right after log, but what about the existing data? How will that be pulled from Airbyte? Will that have a trade-off? By trade-off, I don't mean the compute or the CPU usage or the memory usage that will anyway be running in the clients or my powerful servers, right? That is not an issue, but is there any other speed trade-off or efficiency trade-off or data corruption issues that can happen via Airbyte? Those are the trade-offs that I want to know about.

The **Full Refresh** modes are the simplest methods that Airbyte uses to sync data, as they always retrieve all available information requested from the source, regardless of whether it has been synced before. This contrasts with [**Incremental sync**](/platform/using-airbyte/core-concepts/sync-modes/incremental-append), which does not sync data that has already been synced before.

In the **Overwrite** variant, new syncs will destroy all data in the existing destination table and then pull the new data in. Therefore, data that has been removed from the source after an old sync will be deleted in the destination table.

## Example Behavior[​](#example-behavior "Direct link to Example Behavior")

On the nth sync of a full refresh connection:

## _Replace_ existing data with new data. The connection does not create any new tables.[​](#replace-existing-data-with-new-data-the-connection-does-not-create-any-new-tables "Direct link to replace-existing-data-with-new-data-the-connection-does-not-create-any-new-tables")

data in the destination _before_ the sync:

| Languages |
| --- |
| Python |
| Java |
| Bash |

new data in the source:

| Languages |
| --- |
| Python |
| Java |
| Ruby |

data in the destination _after_ the sync (note how the old value of "bash" is no longer present):

| Languages |
| --- |
| Python |
| Java |
| Ruby |

## Destination-specific mechanism for full refresh[​](#destination-specific-mechanism-for-full-refresh "Direct link to Destination-specific mechanism for full refresh")

The mechanism by which a destination connector accomplishes the full refresh will vary wildly from destination to destination. For our certified database and data warehouse destinations, we will be recreating the final table each sync. This allows us leave the previous sync's data viewable by writing to a "final-table-tmp" location as the sync is running, and at the end dropping the old "final" table, and renaming the new one into place. That said, this may not possible for all destinations, and we may need to erase the existing data at the start of each full-refresh sync.

Typing and deduping | Airbyte Docs 

[Skip to main content](#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

[Try Airbyte Cloud](https://cloud.airbyte.io/signup?utm_campaign=22Q1_AirbyteCloudSignUpCampaign_Trial&utm_source=Docs&utm_content=NavBar)! Free for 30 days, no credit card needed.

Version: Cloud

On this page

AvailableCoreAvailableStandardAvailablePlusAvailableProAvailableEnterprise FlexAvailableSelf-Managed Enterprise [Compare](https://airbyte.com/product/features)

warning

**Typing and Deduping is currently being phased out in favor of [Direct-Load](/platform/using-airbyte/core-concepts/direct-load-tables).**

With Direct Loads, there is no intermediate table result containing the raw JSON blob. Instead, top-level fields are typed during time of insert, and there is only a single destination table maintained per stream.

This page refers to new functionality added by [Destinations V2](/release_notes/upgrading_to_destinations_v2/). Typing and deduping is the default method of transforming datasets within data warehouse and database destinations after they've been replicated. Please check each destination to learn if Typing and Deduping is supported.

## What is Destinations V2?[​](#what-is-destinations-v2 "Direct link to What is Destinations V2?")

[Airbyte Destinations V2](/release_notes/upgrading_to_destinations_v2) provide:

*   One-to-one table mapping: Data in one stream will always be mapped to one table in your data warehouse. No more sub-tables.
*   Improved per-row error/change handling with `_airbyte_meta`: Airbyte will now populate typing changes in the `_airbyte_meta` column instead of failing your sync. You can query these results to audit misformatted or unexpected data.
*   Internal Airbyte tables in the `airbyte_internal` schema: Airbyte will now generate all raw tables in the `airbyte_internal` schema. We no longer clutter your desired schema with raw data tables.
*   Incremental delivery for large syncs: Data will be incrementally delivered to your final tables when possible. No more waiting hours to see the first rows in your destination table.

## `_airbyte_meta` Changes[​](#_airbyte_meta-changes "Direct link to _airbyte_meta-changes")

"Per-row change handling" is a new paradigm for Airbyte which provides greater flexibility for our users. Airbyte now separates `data-moving problems` from `data-content problems`. Prior to Destinations V2, both types of errors were handled the same way: by failing the sync. Now, a failing sync means that Airbyte could not _move_ all of your data. You can query the `_airbyte_meta` column to see which rows failed for _content_ reasons, and why. This is a more flexible approach, as you can now decide how to handle rows with errors/changes on a case-by-case basis.

tip

When using data downstream from Airbyte, we generally recommend you only include rows which do not have an change, e.g:

```
-- postgres syntaxSELECT COUNT(*) FROM _table_ WHERE json_array_length(_airbyte_meta ->> changes) = 0
```

The types of changes which will be stored in `_airbyte_meta.changes`

This is a more flexible approach, as you can now decide how to handle rows with errors/changes on a case-by-case basis.

tip

When using data downstream from Airbyte, we generally recommend you only include rows which do not have an change, e.g:

```
-- postgres syntaxSELECT COUNT(*) FROM _table_ WHERE json_array_length(_airbyte_meta ->> changes) = 0
```

The types of changes which will be stored in `_airbyte_meta.changes` 

…[truncated — use search() for full content]

content.text:
Here's the honest picture on Airbyte's data integrity and speed tradeoffs:

**For existing data (initial sync):** Airbyte does a **Full Refresh** — it `SELECT *` from the source tables you've configured and streams it all to the destination. This works reliably. No data corruption risk here; it's a straightforward read.

**The real tradeoffs worth knowing about:**

### 1. Type coercion / data content issues
Airbyte has a "typing and deduping" layer. When it reads a column from Postgres that's, say, `JSONB` or `NUMERIC(38,18)`, it has to map that to its internal type system. Mismatches don't fail the sync — they get logged in an `_airbyte_meta.changes` column per row. This means **you won't lose data, but some rows may arrive with type warnings** that your ingestion pipeline needs to handle. For Raven this is actually fine — you're chunking text for embeddings, not doing financial arithmetic on decimal precision.

### 2. Deduplication on re-syncs
If a sync fails midway and retries, or if CDC misses a WAL event (e.g., WAL retention expired), Airbyte falls back to full refresh. The dedup layer handles this, but there's a window where **your destination could temporarily have duplicates** until dedup runs. For Raven, this means your chunking pipeline needs to be **idempotent** — if the same row arrives twice, it shouldn't create duplicate embeddings. A simple `ON CONFLICT (source_id, chunk_hash) DO NOTHING` solves this.

### 3. Schema drift
If the client alters their source table (adds/removes/renames columns) between syncs, Airbyte detects this and can propagate, but your downstream pipeline needs to handle it. A column renamed from `description` to `content` means your chunking config that targets `description` would silently get empty values. This requires **schema mapping** in Raven's connector config — "which columns contain the text to chunk" — and validation on each sync.

### 4. Speed — initial bulk load
Airbyte is not the fastest bulk loader. For a 100M row Postgres table, a raw `COPY` command over the wire is faster than Airbyte's stream-and-serialize protocol. But this only matters on the initial full refresh. Once CDC takes over, incremental syncs are near-realtime (seconds to minutes). For Raven's use case — you're going to chunk and embed every row anyway, which is orders of magnitude slower than the data transfer itself — Airbyte's transfer speed is never the bottleneck.

### 5. WAL retention / CDC gap
If the client's Postgres WAL retention is too short (default is sometimes 1 hour) and a sync interval is longer, CDC can miss changes. Airbyte handles this by falling back to full refresh, but that's expensive on large tables. This is a **configuration documentation issue**, not a code issue — your enterprise deployment guide needs to specify minimum WAL retention per source type.

**Summary:**

| Risk | Severity for Raven | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Type coercion edge cases | Low | You're extracting text, not doing math |
| Duplicate rows on retry | Medium | Idempotent chunking pipeline |
| Schema drift | Medium | Schema mapping config + validation per sync |
| Initial bulk speed | None | Embedding is the bottleneck, not transfer |
| CDC WAL gap | Low | Deployment docs specifying retention settings |

None of these are dealbreakers. The key architectural requirement from Raven's side is: **make the chunking/embedding pipeline idempotent and schema-aware**. With that, Airbyte is safe to rely on fully.

Does this address your concern? If so, the next brainstorming question is about the **classification layer** — how admins will map source data to knowledge bases.

# Roadmap

## Phase Overview

| Phase | Scope | Timeline |
|-------|-------|----------|
| **Phase 1: MVP (Chatbot)** | Core API, ingestion pipeline, embeddable chatbot, admin dashboard, SaaS infra | 8-12 weeks |
| **Phase 2: Voice Agent** | LiveKit, STT/TTS, voice sessions, email notifications, smart caching | 4-6 weeks after Phase 1 |
| **v1.0 GA Readiness** | Error tracking, API docs, CDN, status page, virus scanning, secrets mgmt | Alongside Phase 2/3 |
| **Phase 3: WebRTC/WhatsApp** | WhatsApp Business Calling API, LiveKit bridging, browser WebRTC | 4-6 weeks after Phase 2 |
| **Phase 4: Knowledge Graph** | Neo4j, entity extraction, multi-hop queries | Future |
| **Phase 5: Cloud Managed** | AWS Terraform, hosted offering, pricing, i18n, a11y | Future |
| **Edge Optimization** | eBPF-based XDP pre-filtering, kernel-level observability, security audit trail | Future (post-Phase 2) |

## Phase 1 -- MVP (Chatbot)

**Core:** Organization + Workspace + Knowledge Base CRUD, Keycloak auth, PostgreSQL 18 + pgvector

**Ingestion:** File upload (PDF, DOCX, images), URL scraping (Crawl4AI), chunking, multi-provider embeddings (BYOK)

**Retrieval:** Hybrid search (pgvector + BM25 + RRF fusion), reranking

**Interaction:** Embeddable `<raven-chat>` web component with SSE streaming, API key auth

**Dashboard:** Vue.js + Tailwind Plus admin UI, chatbot configurator, test sandbox, analytics

**SaaS Infra:** Stripe billing, SSL/TLS, backups, legal pages, rate limiting, scheduled jobs

## Phase 2 -- Voice Agent + Smart Caching

**Voice:** LiveKit Agents, Deepgram/faster-whisper STT, Cartesia/Piper TTS, Silero VAD

**Email:** Conversation summaries via AWS SES, "resume conversation" links

**Smart Cache:** Semantic response cache (pgvector similarity) to minimize LLM API costs, in-database LLM adaptation for personalized cached responses

## Phase 3 -- WebRTC / WhatsApp

WhatsApp Business Calling API (WebRTC native), LiveKit room bridging, browser "call the assistant" button

## Edge Optimization (Future)

Low-level Linux kernel optimizations using eBPF, targeted at edge/Raspberry Pi deployments. These are performance and security hardening items — not required for MVP or voice agent, but high-value once the platform is stable on hardware.

See `docs/research/ebpf-edge-optimization.md` for full analysis and caveats.

**1. XDP Pre-filtering (Rate Limiting Offload)**
Drop or throttle malicious/excess traffic at the NIC — before the TCP stack, before Gin, before Valkey. Complements the existing application-layer Valkey rate limiter with a kernel-layer first line of defense. Most impactful under DDoS or high-concurrency scenarios on constrained hardware.

**2. Kernel-level Observability (Zero-agent Metrics)**
Collect CPU, memory, syscall, and network metrics via eBPF kprobes/tracepoints directly from the Go binary. Eliminates the need for a separate Prometheus node exporter or monitoring sidecar on the Pi — fits the minimal-footprint edge constraint.

**3. Security Audit Trail (Process + Syscall Monitoring)**
Trace `sys_execve` and socket calls to log every process spawn and outbound connection from the edge node. Supports GDPR/SOC2 audit requirements without a dedicated agent. Can detect anomalous behavior from the AI worker (Python) or other services.

## Milestones

See [GitHub Milestones](https://github.com/ravencloak-org/Raven/milestones) for detailed task tracking.

input.new_string:
## Raven Pro: Enterprise Connectors (Future)

On-premise and hybrid deployment model with data connectors for enterprise clients. Enables "bring your own data" without manual file uploads.

See `docs/research/analytics-observability-research.md` for observability stack details and `docs/research/ebpf-edge-optimization.md` for edge-specific features.

**Deployment Models:**
- Cloud-with-agent: Raven runs in Raven cloud, secure agent on client network connects to their data sources
- On-premise: Full Raven stack deployed inside client infrastructure, data never leaves their network

**Data Connectors (Airbyte-powered):**
- 700+ source connectors via embedded Airbyte (MIT core)
- Databases: PostgreSQL, MySQL, MongoDB, ClickHouse, Oracle, MSSQL, Snowflake, BigQuery
- SaaS: Salesforce, HubSpot, Confluence, Notion, Jira, Google Drive, SharePoint
- Files: S3, GCS, Azure Blob, local filesystem, Parquet
- CDC (Change Data Capture) for real-time incremental sync after initial full refresh
- Raven's ingestion pipeline must be idempotent (dedup on source_id + chunk_hash)

**Data Classification & Knowledge Base Routing:**
- Tier 1: Auto-pull metadata from existing data catalogs (dbt, DataHub, Apache Atlas, Glue)
- Tier 2: LLM-assisted schema inference for raw databases (sample → classify → admin approves)
- Tier 3: Manual labeling UI for simple sources (WordPress, file folders — tree view + drag-and-drop tagging)

**Vector Storage at Scale (ClickHouse + QBit):**
- Phase 1 (MVP): pgvector + ParadeDB (ceiling ~5–10M chunks)
- Phase 2+ (enterprise): ClickHouse + QBit vectors, partitioned by org_id/kb_id (ceiling ~10M chunks per KB)
- Phase 4+ (mega-KBs): Qdrant with scalar quantization only if a single KB exceeds 10M chunks

**Observability (Two-Tier):**
- Cloud/enterprise: SigNoz (ClickHouse-backed, all three signals)
- Edge/self-hosted: OpenObserve (single binary, user-swappable via OTel endpoint env var)

## Edge Optimization (Future)

content.text:
Roadmap updated. Everything captured under "Raven Pro: Enterprise Connectors" — Airbyte, classification tiers, ClickHouse + QBit vector path, observability two-tier model.

Now — the next item you'd raised earlier was the **data classification system**. Specifically: how do we ensure that when data comes in through connectors, it's properly labeled so the knowledge graph knows "this data belongs to this org / this feature / this domain" — without requiring massive manual effort?

You mentioned data lakes/warehouses might already have labeling. Do you want to dig into the classification architecture now, or was there a different topic you wanted to address first?

message.content: Yeah, so you didn't tell me: does ClickHouse plus Qubit make sense for a larger vector embedding as a knowledge database? I am not talking about ClickHouse plus Qubit vector path for observability. I am not sure what that entails, but I was talking about a replacement for Postgres and ParadeDB, perhaps. Whenever the data scale becomes that huge, I am sure it won't be happening anytime soon, but I just like to explore the possibility.

content.text:
To be clear — yes, that's exactly what I meant in the roadmap. The "ClickHouse + QBit vector path" entry is about **replacing pgvector for knowledge base embeddings**, not observability. Let me answer directly.

**Yes, ClickHouse + QBit makes sense as your scaled vector store for RAG.** Here's the concrete comparison:

| | pgvector (now) | ClickHouse + QBit (at scale) |
|---|---|---|
| Index type | HNSW — must fit in RAM | No index needed — scans columnar storage directly |
| 1M chunks | ~8–12 GB RAM for index | ~740 MB peak memory during query |
| 10M chunks | ~80–120 GB RAM — needs dedicated server | Same architecture, scans partition only |
| 100M chunks | Not viable with HNSW | Viable — partition by org/kb, each partition is a small scan |
| Query precision | Fixed at index build time | Tunable per query (read more/fewer bit planes) |
| Pre-filtering | WHERE + vector search in same query — works but slow at scale | Partition pruning on org_id/kb_id, then QBit scans only that partition |
| BM25 keyword search | ParadeDB (excellent) | ClickHouse has full-text indexing too, or keep ParadeDB alongside |

The key insight for your use case: **every RAG query in Raven is scoped** — `WHERE org_id = X AND kb_id = Y`. With ClickHouse partitioned on those keys, QBit never scans the whole table. A 500K chunk knowledge base (one university course) scans in under 300ms. That's fast enough for RAG where the LLM response itself takes 1–3 seconds.

**The migration path is clean:**
- PostgreSQL stays as the relational store (users, orgs, workspaces, API keys, document metadata)
- ParadeDB stays for BM25 keyword search
- Only the **embeddings column** moves to ClickHouse + QBit
- Your Go retrieval service does two parallel queries: BM25 from Postgres, vector from ClickHouse, fuse with RRF — same pattern as today, just a different vector backend

**When to trigger the migration:** When any single tenant's total chunks exceed ~5M and you start seeing pgvector HNSW index builds taking 10+ minutes or RAM pressure on the Postgres instance. That's your signal.

The roadmap entry I wrote is exactly this — I'll fix the wording to make it unambiguous.

# Roadmap

## Phase Overview

| Phase | Scope | Timeline |
|-------|-------|----------|
| **Phase 1: MVP (Chatbot)** | Core API, ingestion pipeline, embeddable chatbot, admin dashboard, SaaS infra | 8-12 weeks |
| **Phase 2: Voice Agent** | LiveKit, STT/TTS, voice sessions, email notifications, smart caching | 4-6 weeks after Phase 1 |
| **v1.0 GA Readiness** | Error tracking, API docs, CDN, status page, virus scanning, secrets mgmt | Alongside Phase 2/3 |
| **Phase 3: WebRTC/WhatsApp** | WhatsApp Business Calling API, LiveKit bridging, browser WebRTC | 4-6 weeks after Phase 2 |
| **Phase 4: Knowledge Graph** | Neo4j, entity extraction, multi-hop queries | Future |
| **Phase 5: Cloud Managed** | AWS Terraform, hosted offering, pricing, i18n, a11y | Future |
| **Raven Pro: Enterprise Connectors** | Airbyte-powered data connectors, on-prem deployment, data classification/labeling, ClickHouse + QBit vectors at scale | Future |
| **Edge Optimization** | eBPF-based XDP pre-filtering, kernel-level observability, security audit trail | Future (post-Phase 2) |

## Phase 1 -- MVP (Chatbot)

**Core:** Organization + Workspace + Knowledge Base CRUD, Keycloak auth, PostgreSQL 18 + pgvector

**Ingestion:** File upload (PDF, DOCX, images), URL scraping (Crawl4AI), chunking, multi-provider embeddings (BYOK)

**Retrieval:** Hybrid search (pgvector + BM25 + RRF fusion), reranking

**Interaction:** Embeddable `<raven-chat>` web component with SSE streaming, API key auth

**Dashboard:** Vue.js + Tailwind Plus admin UI, chatbot configurator, test sandbox, analytics

**SaaS Infra:** Stripe billing, SSL/TLS, backups, legal pages, rate limiting, scheduled jobs

## Phase 2 -- Voice Agent + Smart Caching

**Voice:** LiveKit Agents, Deepgram/faster-whisper STT, Cartesia/Piper TTS, Silero VAD

**Email:** Conversation summaries via AWS SES, "resume conversation" links

**Smart Cache:** Semantic response cache (pgvector similarity) to minimize LLM API costs, in-database LLM adaptation for personalized cached responses

## Phase 3 -- WebRTC / WhatsApp

WhatsApp Business Calling API (WebRTC native), LiveKit room bridging, browser "call the assistant" button

## Raven Pro: Enterprise Connectors (Future)

On-premise and hybrid deployment model with data connectors for enterprise clients. Enables "bring your own data" without manual file uploads.

See `docs/research/analytics-observability-research.md` for observability stack details and `docs/research/ebpf-edge-optimization.md` for edge-specific features.

**Deployment Models:**
- Cloud-with-agent: Raven runs in Raven cloud, secure agent on client network connects to their data sources
- On-premise: Full Raven stack deployed inside client infrastructure, data never leaves their network

**Data Connectors (Airbyte-powered):**
- 700+ source connectors via embedded Airbyte (MIT core)
- Databases: PostgreSQL, MySQL, MongoDB, ClickHouse, Oracle, MSSQL, Snowflake, BigQuery
- SaaS: Salesforce, HubSpot, Confluence, Notion, Jira, Google Drive, SharePoint
- Files: S3, GCS, Azure Blob, local filesystem, Parquet
- CDC (Change Data Capture) for real-time incremental sync after initial full refresh
- Raven's ingestion pipeline must be idempotent (dedup on source_id + chunk_hash)

**Data Classification & Knowledge Base Routing:**
- Tier 1: Auto-pull metadata from existing data catalogs (dbt, DataHub, Apache Atlas, Glue)
- Tier 2: LLM-assisted schema inference for raw databases (sample → classify → admin approves)
- Tier 3: Manual labeling UI for simple sources (WordPress, file folders — tree view + drag-and-drop tagging)

**Vector Storage at Scale (ClickHouse + QBit):**
- Phase 1 (MVP): pgvector + ParadeDB (ceiling ~5–10M chunks)
- Phase 2+ (enterprise): ClickHouse + QBit vectors, partitioned by org_id/kb_id (ceiling ~10M chunks per KB)
- Phase 4+ (mega-KBs): Qdrant with scalar quantization only if a single KB exceeds 10M chunks

**Observability (Two-Tier):**
- Cloud/enterprise: SigNoz (ClickHouse-backed, all three signals)
- Edge/self-hosted: OpenObserve (single binary, user-swappable via OTel endpoint env var)

## Edge Optimization (Future)

Low-level Linux kernel optimizations using eBPF, targeted at edge/Raspberry Pi deployments. These are performance and security hardening items — not required for MVP or voice agent, but high-value once the platform is stable on hardware.

See `docs/research/ebpf-edge-optimization.md` for full analysis and caveats.

**1. XDP Pre-filtering (Rate Limiting Offload)**
Drop or throttle malicious/excess traffic at the NIC — before the TCP stack, before Gin, before Valkey. Complements the existing application-layer Valkey rate limiter with a kernel-layer first line of defense. Most impactful under DDoS or high-concurrency scenarios on constrained hardware.

**2. Kernel-level Observability (Zero-agent Metrics)**
Collect CPU, memory, syscall, and network metrics via eBPF kprobes/tracepoints directly from the Go binary. Eliminates the need for a separate Prometheus node exporter or monitoring sidecar on the Pi — fits the minimal-footprint edge constraint.

**3. Security Audit Trail (Process + Syscall Monitoring)**
Trace `sys_execve` and socket calls to log every process spawn and outbound connection from the edge node. Supports GDPR/SOC2 audit requirements without a dedicated agent. Can detect anomalous behavior from the AI worker (Python) or other services.

## Milestones

See [GitHub Milestones](https://github.com/ravencloak-org/Raven/milestones) for detailed task tracking.
